item.title: WhatsApp phishing campaign abuses fake business docs to install remote access tools
item.content:encoded:
WhatsApp phishing attack uses fake business docs to hack PCs — Should Read

A global phishing campaign is using compromised WhatsApp accounts to send fake business-document VBScript files that, when opened on Windows, silently install ManageEngine Endpoint Central for attacker-controlled remote access. Users should be extremely cautious with unexpected files sent over WhatsApp, even from trusted contacts, and verify them before opening.

  • Kaspersky reported an ongoing campaign in which compromised WhatsApp accounts send heavily obfuscated VBScript files disguised as business and financial documents to contacts.
  • If a victim opens the file on Windows, the script downloads additional files, disables UAC protections through Registry changes, and installs ManageEngine Endpoint Central to give attackers remote administration access.
  • Kaspersky telemetry indicates the campaign has affected users in Brazil, India, Mexico, Singapore, the UK, Spain, Taiwan, Australia, Russia, Vietnam, and Malaysia.
  • The malicious file must be downloaded when delivered through WhatsApp Web, but in the WhatsApp Desktop client it can be executed directly by Windows Script Host.
  • Kaspersky found signs of Chinese language use and infrastructure overlap with IPs previously linked to ValleyRAT and Gh0st RAT activity, but said there is not enough evidence for high-confidence attribution.

item.title: PixelSmash RCE, FortiBleed Credential Harvesting, and ShapedPlugin Backdoor Highlight Supply Chain and AI Agent Threats
PixelSmash FFmpeg RCE (CVE-2026-8461), FortiBleed Credential Sniffer, ShapedPlugin Backdoor, and AI Agent Compromise Vectors
Compromised Argentine Microsoft 365 School Account Phishes U.S. K-12 District
Sapphire Sleet Mastra npm supply-chain attack and Prinz Eugen ransomware emerge
Sapphire Sleet npm Attack, Prinz Eugen Ransomware, and Gravity SMTP CVE-2026-4020 Exploitation
FortiBleed, SocGholish Disruption, AutoJack, and Texas TPWD Breach
item.description: This digest covers a concentrated set of developments involving internet-facing access infrastructure, malware delivery ecosystems, emerging AI-agent attack surfaces, and public-sector data exposure. The most operationally significant items are the reported compromise of tens of thousands of...
item.content:encoded: This digest covers a concentrated set of developments involving internet-facing access infrastructure, malware delivery ecosystems, emerging AI-agent attack surfaces, and public-sector data exposure. The most operationally significant items are the reported compromise of tens of thousands of FortiGate devices in the FortiBleed campaign and the multinational disruption of SocGholish infrastructure, both tied to initial access at scale. CISA warned Fortinet customers about ongoing malicious activity targeting internet-accessible FortiGate appliances, with the FortiBleed campaign reported to have compromised 86,644 devices as of June 19, 2026. SOCRadar attributed the activity to Russian-speaking threat actors and reported that the campaign involved mass scanning for Fortinet remote login endpoints followed by password spraying using known username and password combinations. Generic administrative accounts and built-in Fortinet system accounts reportedly made up most compromised credentials, while organization-specific accounts accounted for 36.7%. In a separate but related initial-access development, Dutch law enforcement and counterparts from Canada, Germany, and the United States disrupted infrastructure associated with SocGholish, also known as FakeUpdates, under Operation Endgame. Authorities took down 106 servers and cleaned infections from 14,971 WordPress websites. SocGholish has operated since 2017 as a JavaScript-based downloader delivered through compromised websites impersonating browser or software updates. The SocGholish ecosystem has been significant because of its role as a conduit into downstream criminal operations. Reported beneficiaries or associated malware families include Evil Corp, LockBit, RansomHub, Dridex, Raspberry Robin, RomCom, Gholoader, MintsLoader, GhostWeaver, AsyncRAT, and NetSupport RAT. Shadowserver also noted use of domain shadowing, where attackers create malicious subdomains under legitimate domains to point to criminal-controlled infrastructure. Microsoft researchers detailed AutoJack, an exploit chain showing how a malicious web page loaded by an AI browsing agent could execute commands on the host running AutoGen Studio. The proof of concept used a Web Content Summarizer agent to launch calc.exe from the AutoGen Studio process, demonstrating host command execution through agent-driven web interaction. Microsoft reported no exploitation in the wild. The issue centered on a vulnerable MCP WebSocket handler present in AutoGen Studio PyPI pre-release builds 0.4.3.dev1 and 0.4.3.dev2, but not in the stable PyPI release 0.4.2.2. The exploit chain relied on a localhost trust check, MCP paths bypassing authentication middleware, and an endpoint that executed a command supplied directly in a request parameter. Maintainers hardened the GitHub main branch in commit b047730, though the article stated that the fix had not yet appeared in a PyPI release. The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a breach affecting 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers through its license system vendor. Exposed information may include driver’s license information, passport numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, and residential addresses. The agency stated that Social Security numbers, dates of birth, and financial information such as credit card data were not impacted. Texas Cyber Command discovered the intrusion and opened an investigation into the extent and impact of the unauthorized access. TPWD said it is working with the vendor to add new safeguards and enhanced monitoring services. The incident highlights continued exposure risk in third-party systems that handle government licensing and identity-related data at large scale.
